meta_title: For Students: project management to Solve can't debug code (Advanced Case Study)
meta_description: Learn how to project management and overcome can't debug code. A Advanced-level Case Study using Antigravity and Claude Code.

content.seo_title: Project Management to Fix Debugging Blocks | AI Builder Guide
content.seo_description: Stop struggling with broken code. Learn how expert project management and AI tools like Antigravity and Claude Code transform your debugging workflow.
content.answer_block: Debugging isn't a code problem; it's a structural one. By implementing rigorous project management—breaking features into atomic tasks and using AI-driven agents like Claude Code—you eliminate cognitive load. You move from chaotic trial-and-error to systematic verification, ensuring every line of code is intentional, testable, and maintainable.
content.intro:
The screen flickers with a wall of red stack traces. You’ve spent six hours chasing a ghost in the machine, and the momentum that fueled your morning has evaporated into pure frustration. This is the 'debugging wall'—the silent killer of student projects and the primary reason most aspiring developers never ship their first SaaS.

But here is the secret: you aren't failing because you lack intelligence; you are failing because you lack a system. Professional engineering isn't about writing perfect code; it's about managing complexity so that bugs have nowhere to hide. By pivoting your approach to structured project management, you turn debugging from a nightmare into a predictable, mechanical process.

steps.title: Decompose the Monolith
steps.description: Break your feature into atomic, testable units. If a function is longer than 20 lines, it is a liability.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Analyze this code block and decompose it into the smallest possible logical units. Identify potential failure states for each unit.
steps.claude_code_command: claude run --task 'Break down the current feature into 5 atomic sub-tasks for tracking'

steps.title: Implement Defensive TDD
steps.description: Write the failure state first. When you know exactly how the code should break, it becomes easier to fix.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Write a unit test for this function that specifically targets the edge case identified in the previous step.

steps.title: Trace the Execution Flow
steps.description: Use AI to map the data flow. Most bugs are caused by unexpected state mutations across files.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Map the execution flow of this function. Where is the state being mutated? Identify the point of divergence.

steps.title: Isolate the Environment
steps.description: Move your logic into a sandbox. If it works in isolation, the bug is in your integration, not your logic.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Extract this logic into a modular, dependency-free helper function.

steps.title: Automate the Regression Check
steps.description: Once fixed, create a guardrail. Never let the same bug enter your codebase twice.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Create a regression test script based on the bug we just fixed to ensure it never recurs.
steps.claude_code_command: claude commit --message 'fix: add regression test for state mutation bug'

pro_tips: Treat every bug as a failure of your documentation, not your syntax.
Use 'Rubber Ducking' with Claude Code; explain the bug out loud to the CLI and let it summarize your logic gaps.
Limit your debugging sessions to 45 minutes; if you haven't found the root cause, reset your environment.
Version control is your undo button; commit before you start a major refactor, regardless of how small.
Monitor memory leaks by running your test suite through a performance profiler alongside your AI agent.
content.next_steps: Integrate these workflows into your next GitHub repository and start tagging your bugs in Issues to track your progress.
content.conversion_block: You now have the framework to stop guessing and start building with professional-grade precision. Don't let bugs define your journey. Ready to build? Join our AI Builder community → Start your first project today.

Write the failure state first. When you know exactly how the code should break, it becomes easier to fix.
Use AI to map the data flow. Most bugs are caused by unexpected state mutations across files.

Once fixed, create a guardrail. Never let the same bug enter your codebase twice.
